rationale: Title X is clean, direct, and covers the three core dimensions of the study (incidence, clinical effects, long-term prognosis) in a logical sequence. The phrase 'long-term prognosis' adds an important dimension absent from Y, which omits any outcome beyond immediate clinical effects. Title Y overloads the reader with procedural detail ('after dry pericardiocentesis'), a specific patient count (86), and a study-design label ('single-center observational cohort study') that JICE convention typically places in the abstract or methods section, not the title. The result is unwieldy and harder to parse at a glance, reducing clarity and appeal without a commensurate gain in information value relevant to the title itself.
loser_weakness: Title Y sacrifices readability and conciseness by embedding the sample size, procedural sub-context, and study design label directly in the title, making it verbose and awkward. It also omits mention of long-term prognosis, leaving the impression the study only describes incidence and acute effects.
